1: Shittim (ha-Shittim, the acacias; also called Abel-Shittim, Num 33:49) was Israel's final encampment east of the Jordan, in the plains of Moab. It is the same staging ground from which Joshua later dispatches the two spies (Josh 2:1) and from which Israel crosses the Jordan (Josh 3:1). The temporal marker “while Israel was dwelling” (vayeshev Yisrael) suggests settled encampment rather than transit — a period of relative stability that made the sexual-religious entanglement possible. The proximity to Moab and Midian placed Israel in range of the Baal Peor fertility cult practiced in the surrounding region.
2: The Moabite women's invitation was religious, not merely social: they invited Israel to “the sacrifices of their gods” (zivchei eloheihen). Sharing a sacrificial meal in the ancient Near East meant participating in the deity's covenant — eating together at the god's table was an act of worship and alliance. Paul makes exactly this point in 1 Cor 10:18-20: those who eat at pagan sacrificial meals “participate in the altar.” Bowing to the Moabite gods followed naturally from eating at their sacrificial tables — sexual access to the women was embedded in the fertility-cult worship of Baal Peor.
3: Vayitsamed Yisrael le-Ba'al Pe'or — Israel “yoked itself” to Baal Peor. The verb tsamed (to yoke, attach, couple) is uniquely strong: it describes the kind of joining used for draft animals under a yoke. Israel did not merely dabble in Peor worship but was bound to it. Baal Peor is the Baal of the mountain Peor (near Shittim) — a local manifestation of the Canaanite storm-and-fertility deity whose worship characteristically involved sexual rites. The Hosea prophecy invokes this incident as Israel's paradigm of covenant betrayal: “Like grapes in the wilderness I found Israel... they came to Baal Peor and consecrated themselves to shame” (Hos 9:10).
4: YHWH's command: “Take all the heads of the people and put them to death publicly before the LORD, in the sight of the sun” (neged hashemesh, before/facing the sun — i.e., in full daylight, publicly, not secretly). The judicial execution of the tribal leaders bears the weight of communal guilt: the leaders failed to prevent the apostasy or participated in it. The phrase “hang them before the LORD” (hoqa' otam la-YHWH) may describe public impalement or exposure (cf. 2 Sam 21:6, 9 where the same verb appears in a ritual execution context), making the punishment a public expiatory act.
5: Moses delegates to the judges of Israel: each judge is to execute those in his jurisdiction who had attached themselves to Baal Peor. The distributive justice — each judge responsible for his own men — is the standard Mosaic judicial structure (Exod 18:21-26). The phrase “those of his men who attached themselves” (ha-nitsmadem le-Va'al Pe'or) uses the same root as v3's “yoked” — the verb marks those who made the religious commitment to Baal. This is targeted execution of the apostate, not mass slaughter.
6: The dramatic juxtaposition: while Moses and the congregation were weeping at the tent of meeting entrance (presumably in penitence for the apostasy and pleading for divine mercy), an Israelite man walked in with a Midianite woman “right in front of Moses and the whole congregation.” The Hebrew liforeihem (before the face of them) emphasizes the brazenness — it was not hidden but public defiance. The phrase “led her to his brothers” (vayaqrev el-echav) or “to his inner chamber” (a possible reading of the rare word) describes the man bringing her into a private space for the sexual-religious act, in plain view of the mourning assembly.
7: Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron is introduced by full genealogy — the Aaronic priestly line is crucial to what follows, since YHWH's response will grant a covenant to Phinehas specifically because of his priestly standing. He “rose from among the congregation” (vayaqom mitoch ha'edah) — his action is not mob violence but a deliberate individual initiative. He takes a spear (romach, a close-combat weapon) and follows the couple. His action echoes the zeal of Levi and Simeon at Shechem (Gen 34) and anticipates the same quality praised in Elijah (1 Kings 19:10, 14) and the zealot tradition in Second Temple Judaism.
8: “And followed the man of Israel into the inner chamber (qubbah, a vaulted tent or domed chamber) and drove it through both of them — the man and the woman through the belly (qobatah).” The word qubbah (from the same root as qobah, vault/dome) may indicate a private inner tent used for the illicit union. Phinehas's single act kills both simultaneously. The physical detail — the spear going through the belly — underscores that the act was not symbolic but lethal. The plague stops immediately: Phinehas's intervention is the expiation that arrests the divine judgment. This is the mechanism of atonement through a human act of judgment — an unusual but OT-attested pattern (cf. Moses interceding by judgment in Exod 32:27-28).
9: The toll: twenty-four thousand died in the plague. Paul cites this incident in 1 Cor 10:8 but gives the number as twenty-three thousand — a discrepancy noted in ancient textual traditions. The most common explanation is that Paul rounds down or that the Hebrew text gives total plague deaths while Paul gives those who died “in one day.” The second census (Num 26:14) shows Simeon's dramatic decline from 59,300 (Num 1:23) to 22,200 — a loss of 37,100 — likely reflecting Simeon's heavy casualties in the Peor plague given that Zimri (the Israelite man killed, v14) was a Simeonite leader.
10: YHWH now addresses Moses directly about Phinehas — not to condemn the extrajudicial killing but to validate it as covenant faithfulness. The divine-speech formula followed by YHWH's approval of Phinehas's action is the interpretive key to the chapter: the narrator does not leave the reader to evaluate Phinehas's act; YHWH evaluates it explicitly and positively. The covenant that follows is YHWH's own validation of what Phinehas did.
11: “Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest has turned my wrath away from the Israelites by being zealous for my cause (beqan'o et-qin'ati, literally, being jealous with my jealousy) in their midst.” The verbal construction qanna + qin'ah (zealous with jealousy) uses the same root YHWH uses to describe his own character: “I YHWH your God am a jealous God” (Exod 20:5, El qanna). Phinehas acted with YHWH's own jealousy for YHWH's own honor — his zeal was not personal passion but divine character expressed through a human agent. This is why YHWH counts it as atonement: Phinehas internalized the divine jealousy.
12: “I am giving him my berit shalom (covenant of peace).” Shalom in covenantal contexts means not merely absence of conflict but completeness, wholeness, and ongoing well-being. The paradox is intentional: Phinehas has just committed a violent act, yet YHWH grants him a covenant of shalom. The peace is between Phinehas and YHWH — a declaration of ongoing divine favor following an act that aligned with divine holiness. It also signals the end of the plague: YHWH's anger has been appeased, and shalom is restored to the community.
13: “It shall be for him and his descendants after him a berit kehunat olam (covenant of perpetual/everlasting priesthood), because he was zealous for his God and made atonement for the Israelites.” The Phinehas covenant extends the Aaronic priestly covenant to Phinehas's line specifically. In later Jewish history, the line of Zadok (Solomon's high priest, 1 Kings 1:35) was traced to Phinehas (1 Chron 6:4-8, 50-53; Ezra 7:1-5). The phrase “made atonement for the Israelites” (vayechapher al-bnei Yisrael) frames Phinehas's act as priestly expiation — he stopped the plague the way a priest's sin offering stops wrath: by interposing himself and his violent action between the community and divine judgment.
14: The identity of the Israelite man: Zimri son of Salu, “a leader of a father's house (nasi beit-av) of the Simeonites.” He was not an ordinary Israelite but a clan chief — someone whose brazen public act carried the authority of his position, making the defiance far more dangerous. The genealogical notation here (unique to the execution narratives) serves a judicial function: the record identifies the guilty party by full name and lineage for the permanent communal memory. Zimri's Simeonite identity connects to the dramatic decline in Simeon's census numbers (ch 26).
15: Cozbi daughter of Zur — her name (from kazav, to lie/deceive) may be a symbolic name or editorial characterization. Zur is identified as “head of a people of a father's house in Midian” — she is not a common woman but the daughter of a Midianite tribal leader, suggesting the Baal Peor episode had an official, politically arranged character (cf. Num 31:16, where Balaam is said to have counseled this very strategy). The revelation that the Midianites orchestrated the seduction — rather than it being an organic cultural encounter — is developed in Num 31.
16: YHWH commands hostility toward Midian. The Moabite women initiated the encounters (v1), but YHWH's judgment focuses on the Midianites — because Midian engineered the apostasy as a weapon (cf. Num 31:16). Moab is excluded from the war mandate (cf. Deut 2:9, where YHWH forbids attacking Moab), but Midian receives it. This distinction tracks with the rest of the Pentateuch's treatment: Moab produces Ruth; Midian is destroyed in the war of Num 31.
17: “Treat the Midianites as enemies (tsaroru et-ha-Midyanim, be hostile to the Midianites) and strike them down.” Tsarar (to be an enemy, to press/besiege) is the same root used for Israel's covenant enemies throughout the Pentateuch. The mandate for war against Midian will be executed in ch 31, Moses's last military act before his death. The verb “strike down” (hikkitem, plural imperative) suggests comprehensive military action, not merely diplomatic hostility.
18: “For they dealt treacherously with you through their schemes in the Peor affair and in the matter of Cozbi, daughter of the Midianite chief, their sister, who was killed on the day of the plague in the matter of Peor.” The word nechilim (schemes, tricks, nochel) indicates deliberate deception rather than organic temptation — the Midianites used the Baal Peor seduction as a calculated weapon against Israel. Num 31:16 confirms: Balaam counseled Balak and the Midianites to use the women to cause Israel to sin. The Peor incident was the military strategy Balaam devised after failing to curse Israel directly (chs. 22-24).
